Q1: Honestly, my study routine was kind of chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside about 18 hours a week, but with nine online courses, I felt like I was constantly jumping from one thing to another. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd listen to lectures while doing other things, but I don't think I was really absorbing everything. My anxiety made it hard to stick to any real schedule because I'd get overwhelmed just looking at all I had to do.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have these bursts of activity right before deadlines, then I'd crash and barely do anything for days. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, logging in every day, and other weeks my anxiety would spike and I'd avoid everything. It wasn't intentional—I just couldn't handle the pressure sometimes, so my schedule became really unpredictable.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline that got me to log in. I'd suddenly remember something was due and rush to the LMS to see what I'd missed. Sometimes I'd get a notification on my phone and that would prompt me. Other times, I'd log in just to check if I'd missed something important because I always had this nagging feeling I was falling behind.
Q4: For a little while, I got into a routine of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee. It felt manageable for maybe two or three weeks. But then assignments started piling up and I started dreading logging in because I knew I'd see all these incomplete tasks. The gamified tasks actually helped briefly—earning points made it feel less overwhelming—but that routine didn't last long.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly my lifeline because I could listen to them multiple times. Since I learn better by hearing things, I'd replay confusing parts over and over. I watched almost three hours of video content because that format just clicks for me. The discussion posts were also helpful when other students explained things in different ways than the professor.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials, honestly. I'd open PDFs and my eyes would just glaze over—I can't learn well from reading alone. I also ignored a lot of the optional practice quizzes because I was so focused on just surviving the required stuff. Looking back, maybe those would have helped my exam scores, but I just didn't have the mental energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or progress. The gamified tasks were actually motivating because I could see points adding up and it felt like I was accomplishing something. If something was going to take forever and I couldn't tell if it would actually help my grade, I'd usually skip it. I needed that instant feedback to feel like my effort mattered.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With my anxiety already through the roof about required work, adding optional stuff felt impossible. Required activities had this weight of fear attached—like if I didn't do them, I'd fail. Optional ones just faded into the background because my brain was in survival mode, not enrichment mode.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of lecture audio to my phone and listened while commuting or doing chores. I also had a study group that met in person, and we'd work through problems together using our notes. I'd record myself summarizing concepts and play them back—that's how I study best. None of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd study for hours using printed notes and audio recordings, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd sit with my study group at the library, talking through concepts out loud, which really helped me understand things. My best learning happened when I was discussing ideas verbally, not clicking through online modules.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download the lecture videos once and then watch them offline repeatedly on my tablet during my commute. I'd also screenshot important slides and review them on my phone throughout the week. The LMS probably showed one view, but I was actually engaging with that content multiple times in ways it couldn't track.
Q12: Around midterms, everything fell apart for me. My anxiety was at its peak, I wasn't sleeping well, and I had extracurricular commitments I couldn't drop. I remember just staring at the LMS login screen and feeling physically sick. The workload felt impossible, and I started avoiding the course entirely for about two weeks, which obviously hurt my grades badly.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just scroll aimlessly without actually doing anything productive. Competing responsibilities from my other eight courses meant I was constantly triaging what needed attention most urgently. My fatigue made it hard to process information even when I was technically 'studying,' so my engagement was really shallow.
Q14: So many times. I'd have the LMS open, clicking through pages, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch videos but realize at the end I couldn't remember anything because I was too anxious about everything else. It was like going through the motions without actually being present. My body was there but my brain had checked out.
Q15: I avoided the bigger assignments constantly, even though I knew they were killing my grade. The anxiety made starting them feel impossible—I'd get this paralysis where I knew it was important but I literally couldn't make myself begin. I'd do smaller, easier tasks first to feel productive, but then run out of time for the important stuff.
Q16: Deadlines controlled everything. I'd cram all my LMS activity into the 24-48 hours before something was due. It wasn't healthy at all, but the pressure of a deadline was the only thing that could cut through my anxiety and actually make me work. Without that urgency, I'd just spiral into avoidance.
Q17: When I got feedback that was encouraging, it actually motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But negative feedback or low scores would send me into an anxiety spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. The gamified feedback with points and badges was easier to handle emotionally than traditional grades. It felt less personal somehow.
Q18: Having nine courses made the structure feel overwhelming rather than helpful. Each course had its own rhythm and deadlines, and I couldn't keep track of everything. The weekly modules were theoretically organized, but with so much competing for my attention, I'd lose track of where I was. The structure existed, but I couldn't follow it consistently.
Q19: The gamified elements actually made engagement easier when I used them—seeing progress bars fill up gave me small wins. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses made everything harder. If there had been more audio-based options or shorter, more digestible chunks, I think I would have engaged more consistently with my learning style.
Q20: That was literally me sometimes. I'd be logged in for hours, clicking around, watching bits of videos, reading discussion posts—but I couldn't bring myself to actually submit work. The anxiety of turning something in and being judged was worse than just passively consuming content. It's like being in a store but being too scared to buy anything.
Q21: I think some people are just really efficient or learn better outside the system. My study group had someone who barely logged in but understood everything from our discussions and her own notes. For me, my offline studying helped, but it wasn't enough to compensate for my low assignment completion. The LMS didn't capture my real effort, but my results still suffered.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when you're active and what you click on, but they completely miss the quality of that engagement. They can't tell if you're anxiously scrolling versus deeply learning. They miss all the offline work, the group discussions, the audio recordings I made. They show behavior but not understanding or emotional state.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it, not just going through motions. It's when I'm curious and asking questions, not just trying to survive. Real engagement is when I lose track of time because I'm interested, not when I'm watching the clock waiting for a video to end so I can check a box.
Q24: The LMS couldn't show how hard I was actually trying despite my anxiety, or how much I learned through listening and discussing with peers. It couldn't capture the tears of frustration or the genuine interest I had in some topics. It also missed that the gamified intervention actually helped my mood and made me feel more hopeful, even if my final grade didn't reflect that improvement.
